title: Accordion fold book
subtitle: A concertina-folded text block with hard covers
excerpt: An accordion book is a single long strip of paper folded back and forth in equal sections, with hard covers glued to the first and last panels. It needs no needle and no thread: the only joining is the fold. The finished piece opens as a sequence of panels or extends into a continuous panorama.
sourceNotes: Accordion and concertina binding forms documented in late-Victorian paper-craft manuals and in Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), notes on folded forms. The accordion fold as a book structure predates codex binding in many East Asian book traditions; the form is referenced in Meiji-era Japanese bookbinding manuals (Internet Archive).

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold pattern where each section folds in the opposite direction from the last, producing a concertina or fan shape that can be extended flat or compressed closed.
A fold where the paper folds toward the folder, creating a valley. In an accordion book, valley and mountain folds alternate.
A fold where the paper folds away from the folder, creating a ridge or mountain. Mountain and valley folds alternate in an accordion book.

projectSchedule.title: Fold the text strip and make the covers
projectSchedule.body: Score, fold, and burnish the accordion strip. Glue the covers.

projectSchedule.title: Press and trim
projectSchedule.body: After overnight drying under weights, trim the foredge if needed.

content.text: An
accordion-fold book
is paper engineering at its simplest: one long strip, folded alternately
valley
and
mountain

, with a hard cover glued to the first and last panels. The book reads as pages when held closed, extends to a panoramic strip when opened fully, and stands upright like a Japanese screen when placed on a table. This pattern makes an A6-panel accordion (eight panels) with 1.5 mm grey-board covers.

attrs.title: Bookbinding adhesives
attrs.body: PVA skins quickly in warm rooms; apply with scrap card from the centre outward, press within sixty seconds, and wipe excess before it dries.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, long-grain, one long strip or joined sections
items.qty: One strip 105 mm tall × 840 mm long for 8 panels at 105 mm each

items.name: Bookbinding board, 1.5 mm, cut to 105 × 107 mm
Cover paper, bookcloth, or decorative paper for covering boards
items.qty: 2 pieces, 135 × 137 mm each (covers boards with 15 mm turn-in)

items.name: Two heavy books for pressing
items.qty: For overnight pressing of covered boards

content.text: Making the text strip

If using a single strip of 840 mm, score the fold lines at 105 mm intervals along the length: seven score lines for eight panels. Score each line with the bone folder against a metal ruler, pressing firmly enough to leave a clear score without breaking the paper fibre. Work from one end to the other.

Fold the strip along each score line in alternating directions. First fold is a valley (fold the paper toward you); the next is a mountain (fold away from you); continue alternating. After each fold, run the bone folder along the crease to sharpen it. Stack the finished accordion neatly and hold under a weight for ten minutes to set the folds.

If the strip must be joined from two sheets: cut the ends of each sheet so the join falls in the middle of a panel, not at a fold. Apply a thin line of PVA 5 mm in from the end of the first strip. Butt the second strip against it and press flat. Allow to dry fully before folding past the join.
Making and attaching the covers

Cut cover boards to 105 × 107 mm (the panel height × the panel width plus 2 mm square). Cover each board in book cloth or decorative paper: apply PVA to the board face, press onto the cover material, mitre the corners, fold turn-ins over, and burnish. Press under heavy books for at least one hour.

Apply PVA to the outside face of the first accordion panel. Centre the back cover board over it, board side down, pressing firmly and burnishing through a clean scrap sheet. Repeat for the last panel with the front cover board. Stack the closed book under weights overnight.
Finishing

After pressing, check that the covers are flush with the text panels on the top and bottom edges. The boards should overhang the foredge by 2 mm on each side. If the text strip is not perfectly even at the foredge, trim with a craft knife against a ruler on the cutting mat, working from front cover through to back.
Care

Store the closed accordion flat or standing upright between bookends. The folds will soften with use over time; the book will still function but will no longer stand as a screen. Keep away from sustained heat, which relaxes the PVA adhesive in the cover panel joints.
Variations
Extend to sixteen panels for a full-length panorama. Join two 840 mm strips at a central panel.
Use watercolour paper (200 gsm hot-press) for the strip to create a portable painting surface. Paint across the full opened length as a continuous landscape.
A dos-à-dos variant houses two accordion books back-to-back in shared covers.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems that arise when making an accordion book.

items.symptom: The folds are uneven and the accordion does not sit flat when closed
items.cause: Score lines were not marked precisely, or the bone folder was not pressed firmly enough at each fold.
items.fix: Mark all fold lines with a fine pencil against the ruler before scoring. Re-burnish each fold with the bone folder, applying firm even pressure from one end to the other.

items.symptom: The cover board warps toward the text strip after gluing
items.cause: PVA moisture caused the paper covering to contract, pulling the board.
items.fix: Press the covered board under a heavy flat weight (two or three large books) for at least thirty minutes immediately after gluing. If already warped, lightly dampen the inside face of the cover and press again.
